name: Olmedo from Ataquines – circular of the Río Adaja
name_original: Olmedo desde Ataquines – circular del río Adaja
region_intro: Road cycling around Ataquines offers routes across a landscape characterized by expansive agricultural fields and gentle rolling hills. The region's topography is suitable for road cyclists seeking relaxed rides. The Adaja River and its environs provide natural elements to the cycling routes. Subtle topographical variations, including the "ataquines" or mounds, contribute to the local terrain. Best road cycling routes around Ataquines The most popular road cycling route is Olmedo from Ataquines – circular of the Río Adaja, an easy 23.4 miles (37.6 km) trail that takes 1 hour 27 minutes to complete. This route allows for exploration of agricultural landscapes and areas surrounding the Adaja River. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Attack from Castillo de La Mota – circular by Las Salinas and Medina del Campo, a moderate 38.1 miles (61.3 km) path. This route traverses expansive fields and passes historical sights like the Castillo de La Mota. Local road cyclists also love the Castle of La Mota – Muriel de Zapardiel loop from Estación de Medina del Campo, a 50.0 miles (80.4 km) trail leading through agricultural landscapes, often completed in about 3 hours 8 minutes. Road cycling around Ataquines is defined by expansive fields, gentle rolling hills, and the Adaja River. The network offers options for varying fitness levels, including easy and moderate routes. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 70 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Ataquines's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Ataquines?
A: There are 9 road cycling routes around Ataquines, offering a variety of options for different skill levels. You can explore routes ranging from easy to moderate difficulty.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling in Ataquines?
A: Road cycling in Ataquines is characterized by expansive agricultural fields and gentle rolling hills, often referred to as 'suaves cuestas alomadas'. The topography is generally mild, making it suitable for relaxed and enjoyable rides.

Q: Are there easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Ataquines?
A: Yes, Ataquines offers 2 easy road cycling routes perfect for beginners or those seeking a relaxed ride. One popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1153308064/olmedo-from-ataquines-circular-of-the-rio-adaja" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Olmedo from Ataquines – circular of the Río Adaja&lt;/a&gt;, which is 37.6 kilometers long and primarily on asphalt.

Q: Are there more challenging road cycling routes around Ataquines?
A: While the region is known for its gentle terrain, there are 7 moderate road cycling routes for those looking for a bit more distance and varied landscapes. These routes traverse expansive fields and may include slightly more elevation gain.

Q: What scenic viewpoints or natural features can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: Many routes offer picturesque views of vast agricultural fields and the environs of the Adaja River. You might also notice the subtle topographical variations from the 'ataquines' or mounds that give the town its name. For a mix of nature and history, consider routes near the Zapardiel River to see old mills, part of the historical Ruta de los Molinos.

Q: Are there any historical sights or landmarks to explore while cycling near Ataquines?
A: Yes, several routes pass by or are near historical attractions. You can observe the prominent tower of the baroque Church of San Juan Bautista in Ataquines, built on the remains of a 17th-century castle, or the hermitage of San Roque. Routes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1153266881/attack-from-castillo-de-la-mota-circular-by-las-salinas-and-medina-del-campo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Attack from Castillo de La Mota – circular by Las Salinas and Medina del Campo&lt;/a&gt; pass historical sights like the Castillo de La Mota. Other nearby historical highlights include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3505363" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Historic Center of Olmedo&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5587174" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roman Villa of Almenara&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in Ataquines?
A: Yes, many of the routes around Ataquines are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1153308064/olmedo-from-ataquines-circular-of-the-rio-adaja" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Olmedo from Ataquines – circular of the Río Adaja&lt;/a&gt;, which is an easy loop.

Q: What is the best season for road cycling in Ataquines?
A: The region's climate generally makes spring and autumn ideal for road cycling, offering pleasant temperatures and less extreme weather. Summer can also be enjoyable, particularly during the cooler parts of the day, while winter might be colder but still offers rideable days.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about the routes in Ataquines?
A: Over 70 road cyclists have explored Ataquines using komoot, indicating a good level of interest in the area. Cyclists often appreciate the region's gentle topography and the opportunity to explore agricultural landscapes and riverine environments on well-maintained asphalt roads.

Q: Are there family-friendly road cycling options in Ataquines?
A: Given the region's gentle rolling hills and easy routes, many of the paths are suitable for families. The easy, circular routes, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1153308064/olmedo-from-ataquines-circular-of-the-rio-adaja" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Olmedo from Ataquines – circular of the Río Adaja&lt;/a&gt;, are particularly well-suited for a family outing.

Q: Where can I find parking for road cycling routes in Ataquines?
A: While specific parking areas are not detailed for every route, Ataquines is a small town, and you can typically find parking in or near the town center, or at the start points of popular routes. It's advisable to check local regulations upon arrival.

Q: Are there cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the routes?
A: As Ataquines is an agricultural area, amenities like cafes might be concentrated in the main town or larger villages along the routes, such as Medina del Campo or Olmedo. It's a good idea to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially on longer rides, and plan stops in advance.
tips: Ataquines is a small agricultural town on the northern plateau, near the border with the provinces of Ávila and Segovia. Like many rural towns in Spain, the church is the most notable monument in the city with a tall tower visible from a distance.

Once a walled city, Olmedo retains some of its medieval charm, dating back to the Castilian and Leonese Mudejar period with several churches and heritage buildings located near the Plaza Mayor. It is a good place to stop for a coffee and admire the history.

waypoints.name: Olmedo City Walls and Historic Center
